imageCaption: Anthony's worn roll-up pegboard of miniature license plates catches afternoon light from two time zones at once, and one plate—a bright turquoise rectangle from somewhere that doesn't exist yet—glows slightly brighter than the others.
text:
Anthony discovered it while organizing his pegboard.

This was not unusual. Anthony organized his pegboard the way other people meditated—methodically, peacefully, with the quiet satisfaction of a man who had found a system that worked. He'd been collecting license plates since before Aaliyah was born, which meant the pegboard had evolved from a small cork square to the current sprawling fabric contraption that he rolled up and stored with the reverence of someone protecting a national treasure.

What was unusual was that one of the plates was glowing.

"Dad," Aaliyah said, not looking up from her notebook where she was trying to map the café's temporal geometry for the fifteenth time, "why is the Curaçao plate on fire?"

"It's not on fire," Anthony said, but he was already moving it away from the table and into better light. "It's just... luminescent. See? The edges are—"

He held it up to the window that was simultaneously displaying Lisbon's 2026 afternoon and Toronto's early morning, and the turquoise rectangle—definitely a Curaçao plate, with its distinctive color and the three cross-marks representing the three islands—began to glow brighter.

Luisana set down her coffee. Her journalist instincts, apparently, were still active even in temporal crisis situations.

"How long has it been doing that?" she asked.

"Just started. I was organizing and—" Anthony turned the plate in his hands, watching the light shift. "Actually, I don't think this one was glowing yesterday. Or the day before. I think it just... started."

Elijah appeared at Anthony's elbow with the speed of a child who possessed some kind of supernatural sense for when something unusual was happening. Sully, of course, appeared with him, because Sully appeared everywhere Elijah went, which was how the dog had managed to steal food on three continents without anyone being able to accurately assign blame.

"The plate is trying to tell us something," Elijah announced with absolute certainty.

"The plate is not trying to tell us anything," Aaliyah said automatically, which was the correct older-sibling response to approximately eighty percent of Elijah's theories. "It's probably a glitch side effect. Everything's sideways right now. Maybe light behaves differently when it has to travel through two time zones simultaneously."

But she was already moving toward her father anyway, notebook abandoned, because the rational explanation and the *correct* explanation were increasingly turning out to be different things.

The plate continued to glow.

"There's another one," Luisana said quietly.

Anthony spread his pegboard flat across the table—carefully, mindfully, like he was unrolling something sacred—and they could all see it: another plate, this one from somewhere with a flag Aaliyah didn't immediately recognize, was also faintly luminescent. Less bright than the Curaçao plate, but definitely glowing.

And then a third began to brighten.

"Dad, how many plates do you have?" Aaliyah asked, already knowing the answer was going to be a lot because her father collected things the way other people breathed—naturally, continuously, without really thinking about it.

"Two hundred and thirty-four," Anthony said. "I've been doing this for seventeen years. I get one from everywhere we go. And some places before that, when your mom and I were younger, when we thought we were going to be more spontaneous about travel and less—"

He stopped. Another plate brightened. Then another.

Maria, who had been sitting with Catarina in the corner—they'd barely moved since yesterday morning, just existing in the same space with the intensity of people who'd lost three hundred and forty-seven days and had no intention of wasting another second—stood up. She walked over to the pegboard and pointed.

"That one," she said. "That one's brighter than the others."

It was true. Among the growing constellation of luminescent plates, one stood out: a pale cream-colored rectangle with blue lettering, clearly from somewhere in the Mediterranean, and glowing with the intensity of a small star.

Aaliyah's hand moved automatically to her sketch pad. She didn't know why. But something about the pattern felt important, felt like it was trying to organize itself into meaning the way all the other glitches had tried to organize themselves into meaning.

"These aren't all at the same brightness," she said slowly, tracing the pattern with her pencil. "Look—they're getting brighter in a specific sequence. Like they're showing us a path."

Her father looked at her with that expression that meant he was about to acknowledge something he'd been thinking about but hadn't quite said aloud yet.

"A path to where?" Luisana asked, but she was already opening her notebook, already flipping back through eight days of observations, and Aaliyah could see her mother doing what her mother did best—finding the pattern inside the chaos, the story inside the data.

Anthony held the brightest plate—the cream-colored Mediterranean one—up to the light again. In that doubled illumination, from both Lisbon and Toronto simultaneously, Aaliyah could see something written on the back, something that had always been there but that nobody had ever thought to look for.

Coordinates.

And below them, a single word in handwriting Aaliyah recognized immediately: *Abuela*.

The café held very still.

Sully made a small sound that might have been a bark or might have been acknowledgment, and somewhere outside—in the impossible space between two cities—a morpho butterfly, vivid iridescent blue and unmistakable, landed on the window frame and folded its wings like it was waiting.
